title: Move DIV/REM off ALU critical path
motivation:
The current one-cycle ALU contains real SystemVerilog division and remainder
operators in the same EX-stage result cone as common integer operations.
Those operators are expensive on the Tang Nano 20K fabric and likely depress
Fmax, while CoreMark's timed region should execute DIV/REM rarely or not at
all. Improving Fmax without touching the common ADD/branch/load/store path is
therefore a promising fitness win.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current one-cycle ALU contains real SystemVerilog division and remainder

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Split DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU out of the combinational ALU into a small bounded
iterative divider. The in-order pipeline should stall only when one of those
rare operations is in EX, then write the completed result through the existing
EX/MEM path. Keep MUL/MULH variants one-cycle and keep the RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS
substitutions combinational so formal remains tractable. The expected Fmax and
LUT improvement should outweigh the occasional divider latency penalty on
CoreMark.

title: Move DIV/REM to multicycle EX unit
motivation:
The current ALU contains combinational signed and unsigned division and
remainder logic in the single-cycle EX path. On a small FPGA this is likely
a major LUT and timing cost even though CoreMark's hot loops are dominated by
adds, compares, loads, stores, branches, and multiplies rather than divides.
Retiming only DIV/REM into a small multicycle unit should raise median Fmax
and reduce routing pressure while preserving single-cycle behavior for the
common integer operations.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Split RV32M division and remainder out of rtl/alu.sv into a dedicated
iterative divider/remainder unit issued by EX. When EX sees DIV, DIVU, REM, or
REMU, it should hold IF/ID/EX, keep the operation's operands and RVFI payload
stable, and release one EX/MEM result only when the unit completes. MUL/MULH
operations remain in the existing combinational ALU so CoreMark's multiply
throughput is not sacrificed. The added rare-operation stalls should cost few
CoreMark cycles, while the shorter common EX critical path should improve the
Fmax side of the fitness metric.

title: Add BTFNT fetch predictor
motivation: The current five-stage core always fetches sequentially and only redirects when a branch or jump reaches EX, so every taken CoreMark loop branch pays a multi-cycle wrong-path bubble. CoreMark is loop-heavy, and the present bench history has no prior predictor attempts, making branch penalty reduction the cleanest predictor-side opportunity.

hypothesis.motivation: The current five-stage core always fetches sequentially and only redirects when a branch or jump reaches EX, so every taken CoreMark loop branch pays a multi-cycle wrong-path bubble. CoreMark is loop-heavy, and the present bench history has no prior predictor attempts, making branch penalty reduction the cleanest predictor-side opportunity.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Add a small static fetch predictor that predicts JAL and backward conditional branches taken, predicts forward conditional branches not taken, and carries the predicted next PC with the instruction into EX. EX should redirect only when the resolved next PC differs from the predicted next PC, eliminating the normal taken-backward-loop penalty while preserving exact RVFI pc_wdata, trap behavior, and the nret=1 port contract. This should improve CoreMark cycles with modest LUT/critical-path risk because it avoids tables and uses only IF-side opcode/offset decode plus one predicted-PC field in the pipeline bundles.

title: Share MUL hardware in ALU
motivation:
The current post-divider core still computes three independent 64-bit
multiply products in the combinational ALU: signed*signed, unsigned*unsigned,
and signed*unsigned. CoreMark keeps MUL single-cycle useful, but carrying
three multiplier cones through the Tang Nano 20K fabric likely costs LUTs,
routing, and Fmax even though only one MUL variant can retire at a time.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current post-divider core still computes three independent 64-bit
multiply products in the combinational ALU: signed*signed, unsigned*unsigned,
and signed*unsigned. CoreMark keeps MUL single-cycle useful, but carrying
three multiplier cones through the Tang Nano 20K fabric likely costs LUTs,
routing, and Fmax even though only one MUL variant can retire at a time.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the three parallel multiplier products in rtl/alu.sv with one
sign-configurable 33x33 multiplier. Select operand sign extension from the
ALU opcode, use the low 32 bits for MUL, and use product bits 63:32 for
MULH/MULHU/MULHSU. This should preserve all RV32M results and single-cycle
multiply latency while shrinking the EX-stage arithmetic cone and improving
median FPGA Fmax without changing CoreMark instruction count.

title: Share M-extension multiplier datapath
motivation:
The successful structural change already removed DIV/REM from the common EX
critical path, leaving the ALU's parallel multiplier bank as a likely large
timing and routing cost. The current ALU computes signed*signed,
unsigned*unsigned, and signed*unsigned 64-bit products in parallel even
though only one MUL-family result can be selected for a retiring instruction.
CoreMark likely uses MUL often enough that making multiplication multicycle is
risky, but sharing the single-cycle datapath should reduce LUT/DSP routing
pressure without adding any stalls.

title: Add tiny EX-updated BTB
motivation: The current core predicts every control-flow instruction as not taken and only redirects in EX, so hot CoreMark loop branches and direct jumps pay wrong-path bubbles repeatedly. The prior BTFNT predictor tried to remove those bubbles but added opcode decode, immediate extraction, and target adders to the IF/PC path, which likely consumed the cycle savings as Fmax loss.

hypothesis.motivation: The current core predicts every control-flow instruction as not taken and only redirects in EX, so hot CoreMark loop branches and direct jumps pay wrong-path bubbles repeatedly. The prior BTFNT predictor tried to remove those bubbles but added opcode decode, immediate extraction, and target adders to the IF/PC path, which likely consumed the cycle savings as Fmax loss.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Add a very small direct-mapped branch target buffer that is read in IF and updated only from resolved control-flow instructions in EX. Each entry stores valid, a compact PC tag, a resolved target, and a 2-bit taken counter; IF predicts taken only on tag hit with a taken counter and otherwise preserves the current sequential fetch behavior. This targets the same CoreMark loop-branch penalty as BTFNT while avoiding IF-side instruction decode and target addition, replacing them with a narrow tag compare and stored-target mux on the PC path.

title: Source-aware load-use stalls
motivation:
The current hazard unit stalls after every load when the following
instruction's raw rs1 or rs2 bit fields match the load destination. That is
conservative but over-stalls instructions whose matching field is actually
an immediate, destination, or unused encoding field, such as OP-IMM, LOAD,
LUI, AUIPC, JAL, FENCE, and EBREAK. CoreMark is load-heavy, so removing
these false load-use bubbles can improve iterations per cycle without
touching the already-sensitive fetch prediction or multiplier paths.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Teach the load-use interlock which source operands the IF/ID instruction
really consumes, then qualify the existing rd comparisons with use_rs1 and
use_rs2. This keeps the mandatory one-cycle stall for true load consumers
such as OP, STORE, BRANCH, JALR, and LOAD base-address dependencies, while
allowing non-consuming or immediate-field matches to proceed. The change is
a small decode-side micro-optimization: it should reduce CoreMark cycles and
add only simple opcode checks off the critical IF PC path, preserving the
nret=1 RVFI contract and all trap semantics.

title: Add sequential prefetch FIFO
motivation:
The current front end directly couples decode consumption to raw imemReady: every
instruction-memory stall injects a bubble, and backend stalls also stop the PC
even when the instruction bus could have fetched useful sequential work. Prior
predictor attempts showed that adding target decode/BTB logic on the IF path can
lose Fmax, so the safer structural opportunity is to decouple fetch from decode
without predicting control flow.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current front end directly couples decode consumption to raw imemReady: every
instruction-memory stall injects a bubble, and backend stalls also stop the PC
even when the instruction bus could have fetched useful sequential work. Prior
predictor attempts showed that adding target decode/BTB logic on the IF path can
lose Fmax, so the safer structural opportunity is to decouple fetch from decode
without predicting control flow.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a tiny two-entry fall-through sequential prefetch FIFO in the IF stage that
stores fetched {pc,instr} pairs. When ID/EX is stalled by load-use, dmem, or DIV
backpressure, the IF side should keep requesting sequential instructions while
FIFO space remains; when imemReady drops but the FIFO is non-empty, decode can
continue from the buffered head instead of taking an immediate bubble. On any
EX redirect, flush the FIFO and restart fetch at the resolved target, preserving
the existing no-predictor control-flow semantics and exact RVFI pc/instruction
ordering. This should improve CoreMark iterations/cycle under the 22 percent
randomized iStall+dStall methodology while adding only small FIFO control muxes,
not branch target adders or BTB lookup logic, to the front end.

title: Pipeline lookahead BTB
motivation: The core still fetches sequentially until control flow resolves in EX, so hot CoreMark loop branches and direct jumps pay wrong-path bubbles. Prior predictor attempts showed that the main risk is not correctness but Fmax: IF-side BTFNT decode/target adders and same-cycle BTB lookup logic lengthened the PC feedback path enough to erase cycle savings. The remaining promising predictor angle is to keep dynamic branch learning while moving lookup work off the PC critical path.

hypothesis.motivation: The core still fetches sequentially until control flow resolves in EX, so hot CoreMark loop branches and direct jumps pay wrong-path bubbles. Prior predictor attempts showed that the main risk is not correctness but Fmax: IF-side BTFNT decode/target adders and same-cycle BTB lookup logic lengthened the PC feedback path enough to erase cycle savings. The remaining promising predictor angle is to keep dynamic branch learning while moving lookup work off the PC critical path.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Add a tiny direct-mapped lookahead BTB whose tag/counter/target lookup is performed for the next sequential PC and registered one cycle before that PC reaches IF. The PC update path should then choose between EX redirect, the already-registered prediction for the current PC, and pc+4; no same-cycle table read, tag compare, counter check, or target read feeds the PC register. EX updates the table on resolved conditional branches and direct JALs, carries the predicted next PC with each instruction, and redirects only when the resolved next PC differs from the carried prediction. This should recover repeated taken-loop and direct-jump bubbles while avoiding the Fmax loss seen in the prior IF-side predictors.

title: Hazard-only source-use interlock
motivation:
The current load-use detector stalls whenever a load destination matches either
raw source field in the following instruction, even when that field is actually
an immediate, destination, or otherwise unused encoding bit. CoreMark is
load-heavy, and the previous source-aware attempt failed from changing decoded
source/RVFI fields rather than from the interlock idea itself.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a small hazard-only predecode for the IF/ID instruction's real source
operands and qualify the load-use rd comparisons with those bits. Keep the raw
rs1/rs2 addresses and values in the pipeline exactly as encoded so cosim and
RVFI continue to see the architectural instruction fields, while only the stall
decision skips false dependencies for OP-IMM, LOAD, LUI, AUIPC, JAL, FENCE, and
EBREAK-style non-consuming forms. This should reduce unnecessary bubbles with
little timing risk because it touches only the existing hazard decision and adds
no predictor, FIFO, multiplier, or critical fetch-target machinery.

title: Add conservative store buffer
motivation:
Prior structural and predictor attempts show that adding more IF-side control
hurts this core, while the benchmark still runs with randomized dmem stalls.
Stores currently freeze the whole in-order pipeline whenever dmemReady is low,
even though normal memory stores produce no register result and many following
ALU/control instructions do not need the data port immediately.

hypothesis.motivation:
Prior structural and predictor attempts show that adding more IF-side control
hurts this core, while the benchmark still runs with randomized dmem stalls.
Stores currently freeze the whole in-order pipeline whenever dmemReady is low,
even though normal memory stores produce no register result and many following
ALU/control instructions do not need the data port immediately.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a one-entry store buffer in the MEM side that accepts aligned normal-memory
stores when dmemReady is low, retires the store through the existing MEM/WB and
RVFI path, and drains the buffered write on the dmem port as soon as the bus is
ready. Keep all MMIO stores, including CoreMark start/stop markers and UART, on
the current blocking path so externally observed timing is unchanged. While the
buffer is full, stall younger loads and stores until it drains, but allow younger
non-memory instructions to keep flowing. This targets CoreMark cycles lost to
random store backpressure without adding branch prediction, fetch FIFO, or other
front-end critical-path logic.

title: Add one-entry loop predictor
motivation:
The core still pays an EX-stage redirect bubble on every taken loop branch and direct jump.
Prior predictor attempts lost fitness because IF-side instruction decode, target adders,
BTB arrays, tag/counter lookup, or broad prediction metadata increased front-end timing
cost more than they saved in CoreMark cycles. A narrower predictor should target only the
hottest repeated taken control-flow PCs while keeping the PC path close to the current
sequential fetch design.

hypothesis.motivation:

cost more than they saved in CoreMark cycles. A narrower predictor should target only the

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a single-entry last-taken target predictor for conditional branches and direct JALs.
The entry stores one full PC tag, one resolved target, and a tiny 2-bit confidence counter;
IF predicts taken only on an exact tag hit with the counter MSB set, otherwise it fetches
pc+4 exactly as today. EX carries and compares the predicted next PC against the resolved
next PC, redirecting only on mismatch, so correct hot-loop predictions avoid the normal
taken-branch bubble while loop exits are repaired precisely. This avoids IF opcode decode,
immediate generation, multi-entry BTB muxing, and lookahead-table plumbing, trading one
comparator and one target mux for fewer CoreMark loop-control cycles.

title: Case decode memory lanes
motivation:
The current MEM stage extracts load bytes with a variable right shift and
builds byte masks with variable left shifts. Even though the shift amount is
only two address bits, FPGA synthesis can still leave a deeper mux/shifter
cone on the dmem read-data to MEM/WB path and on store mask generation.
CoreMark is load/store heavy, so trimming this path should improve Fmax
without adding control state or changing the instruction schedule.

title: Add MEM-to-EX load bypass
motivation:
The recent hazard-only source-use interlock removed false load-use stalls, but
true load-use pairs still always pay a bubble even when the data memory is
ready. CoreMark is load-heavy, and the benchmark injects randomized dmem
stalls, so letting a dependent instruction wait in EX and consume the load as
soon as the MEM-stage data is available can improve iterations per cycle
without adding the front-end predictor, FIFO, or store-buffer structures that
regressed in prior rounds.

title: Retiming divider final fixup
motivation:
The current best core already moved DIV/REM out of the common ALU path, but
the iterative divider still has a likely long registered path on its final
iteration: restoring compare/subtract, quotient-bit insertion, optional
quotient or remainder negation, result muxing, and result_q capture all sit
in one cycle. CoreMark should execute DIV/REM rarely in the timed region, so
paying one extra divider-only cycle is a good trade if it raises FPGA Fmax.

and canonical JALR returns are always-taken control-flow instructions whose

title: Register IF/ID fetch boundary
motivation:
The current front end has no real IF/ID register: io_imemData feeds the
decoder, immediate generator, register-file read addresses, hazard predecode,
and then the ID/EX register in the same cycle. In the FPGA Fmax harness that
path is driven by a live LFSR input, so it is a plausible remaining timing
cone after the successful MEM/WB writeback retime. Prior structural attempts
that added prediction, FIFOs, store buffers, or same-cycle load bypassing
regressed because they added control or long bypass paths; a plain fetch
boundary register is a narrower retime.

that stays local to EX can target Fmax by removing canonical-return JALR target
calculation from the same-cycle redirect path without adding another fetch-stage
predictor.

Add a tiny EX-local return target predictor for canonical returns. Track two
return addresses from resolved JAL/JALR call instructions in EX, and when EX sees a
valid canonical return such as JALR x0, 0(x1), drive the existing redirect target
from the predicted return address rather than from the forwarded rs1+imm adder.

detection, and a registered next-cycle repair if the prediction was wrong or the
target was misaligned. Route non-return JALR instructions through the same delayed
redirect path so the forwarded rs1+imm adder no longer feeds the same-cycle PC
update mux; leave conditional branches and direct JALs unchanged. This avoids the
failed IF/ID predictor pattern while potentially improving Fmax on the EX-to-IF
redirect cone and preserving precise nret=1 RVFI ordering.

title: Remove regfile reset fanout
motivation:
The current best core has avoided wins from extra front-end control, but it
still synchronously resets all 32 integer registers with a for-loop. That
creates a 1024-bit reset cone and can prevent the register array from being
mapped as an FPGA memory-style resource. CoreMark does not benefit from
clearing every integer register after reset; x0 is already hardwired to zero
and the pipeline/control state can remain normally reset.

synchronously cleared by reset. Keep x0 hardwired through the existing read
and write gating, and use power-up initialization only if deterministic
simulation startup is needed rather than carrying a live reset mux on every
bit. This should reduce reset fanout and give Yosys/nextpnr more freedom to
implement the 2-read/1-write register file as distributed memory or at least
as flops without reset muxes, improving Fmax without changing CPI, forwarding,
memory behavior, trap behavior, or the nret=1 RVFI port contract.

title: Valid-gated pipeline bubbles
motivation:
The recent structural winners were narrow retimes that removed existing muxing,
while predictors, FIFOs, store buffers, load bypassing, and extra fetch staging
regressed by adding new control on sensitive paths. The current pipeline still
flushes or bubbles whole packed ID/EX and EX/MEM payloads to zero, which puts
high-fanout load-use, redirect, and divider-busy controls on many data bits that
are architecturally irrelevant when valid is low.

their previous values when invalid, and gate EX redirects and MEM requests with
the stage valid bit. This keeps the five-stage schedule, forwarding behavior,
trap rules, and nret=1 RVFI contract unchanged while reducing wide mux/reset
pressure on the inter-stage register cones.

title: Shadowed hot-taken predictor
motivation:
CoreMark still loses cycles on repeated taken loop branches and direct jumps,
but every prior predictor that added IF/ID target arithmetic, BTB lookup
metadata, or return-stack repair logic has regressed. The remaining predictor
opportunity is to keep the front-end prediction state extremely narrow and
avoid carrying a full predicted next PC through the existing pipeline bundles.

Add a one-entry hot-taken predictor for static branch/JAL instructions that
stores the resolved PC, full instruction word, and resolved aligned target
after EX observes a taken static control-flow instruction. IF predicts taken
only when both the current PC and fetched instruction exactly match the entry,
so it needs no opcode decode, immediate generation, or target adder on the
fetch path. Carry only a one-bit predicted-taken marker into EX; because the
entry is keyed by the exact instruction word, EX can recompute the same static
target and suppress the normal redirect on correct predictions, or repair loop
exits by redirecting to pc+4. Clear or weaken the entry on a matching not-taken
conditional branch. This targets the hottest repeated taken PCs while avoiding
the full predicted-next-PC fields and broad repair plumbing that hurt earlier
predictor attempts.

title: Add redirect target replay predictor
motivation:
The bench history shows that conventional branch predictors have regressed
because they put decode, target adders, BTB/RAS lookup, instruction matching,
or full prediction repair metadata on timing-sensitive front-end paths. The
remaining predictor opportunity is to recover some taken-control-flow cost
without predicting the next PC before EX resolves the branch.

CoreMark is loop-heavy and the apples-to-apples cosim injects randomized
instruction-memory stalls. A hot taken branch repeatedly fetches the same
redirect target, so remembering the target instruction can avoid target-fetch
iStall bubbles while leaving the existing not-taken front end and EX redirect
semantics intact.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a one-entry redirect-target replay predictor in IF. When a taken branch
or jump redirects to a target and the target instruction is later fetched
successfully, remember {target_pc, target_instr}. On later fetches of the
same PC, especially immediately after another EX redirect, allow IF to supply
the remembered instruction and treat the fetch as internally ready even if
io_imemReady is low. The external imem address can remain the architectural PC
and the cache can refresh whenever the real bus is ready.

This does not speculate control flow earlier, so it avoids the failed PC-mux
predictor pattern and requires no branch outcome counters, target adders,
instruction-word match on the next-PC path, or misprediction repair metadata.
Correctness is preserved because instruction memory is immutable in the bench
contract, RVFI still retires exactly the fetched PC/instruction pair, and EX
remains the only branch/jump resolution point. The expected gain is modest but
targeted: fewer repeated loop-target fetch stalls under the 22 percent iStall
model with much lower Fmax risk than another BTB/RAS predictor.

title: Drop dead LUI control bit
motivation:
The strongest recent win came from narrowing stage-local control payloads, which suggests packed-control routing and reset/flush muxing are still important on this FPGA.  The current ID/EX control bundle still carries an `is_lui` bit from the decoder even though execute never consumes it; LUI behavior is already fully encoded by `alu_op = ALU_LUI`, `alu_src = 1`, and the U-immediate.

This is a narrower follow-up than removing larger payload fields such as the EX/MEM branch metadata that previously regressed.  It targets one demonstrably dead decode/control signal with no CPI, RVFI, memory, divider, or branch-policy changes.

title: Direct-JAL target memo predictor
motivation:
The bench-core predictor history shows that conditional branch prediction,
BTBs, RAS schemes, exact-instruction matching, and target replay have all
tended to lose more Fmax than they recover in CoreMark CPI. The remaining
useful predictor target is direct JAL: it is always taken, has a stable
resolved target after first execution, and needs no branch-condition compare,
counter update policy, return stack, or instruction-side immediate decode in
the fetch path.

title: Remove IF NOP injection mux
motivation:

The reliable structural wins have instead removed existing muxing and packed-control fanout without changing CPI. The current IF stage still rewrites every fetched instruction through a 32-bit NOP mux on redirect or imem stall so the hazard unit and ID/EX controls see a harmless bubble.

The pipeline already carries an IF/ID valid bit, so the same bubble semantics can be represented with valid plus explicit side-effect-control zeroing in ID instead of modifying the instruction word before decode.

The pipeline already carries an IF/ID valid bit, so the same bubble semantics can be represented with valid plus explicit side-effect-control zeroing in ID instead of modifying the instruction word before decode.

Stop forcing IF/ID instr to ADDI x0,x0,0 on flush, redirect, and imem stall. Let if_stage pass the fetched instruction bits through unchanged while marking the IF/ID payload invalid, then make id_stage register a side-effect-free control word whenever in.valid is low. Add IF/ID valid qualification to the load-use hazard predecode so invalid fetch data cannot create a false stall.

This is narrower than the failed full valid-gated bubble attempt: it does not change EX/MEM or MEM/WB bubble policy, does not add stage-valid terms to forwarding or MEM request cones, and does not alter branch prediction, memory ordering, RVFI retirement, or the nret=1 port contract. The expected win is a shorter front-end instruction-data path and less 32-bit mux fanout with no CoreMark CPI cost.

title: Predict imem stall replay hits
motivation:
Prior branch predictors for this core repeatedly regressed because they put
target lookup, target arithmetic, instruction matching, or wide repair
metadata on the PC redirect path. CoreMark is still evaluated with randomized
instruction-memory stalls, and hot loop bodies repeatedly refetch the same
small set of instruction PCs. A predictor that only speculates on the fetched
instruction word during an imem stall can target CPI without predicting a new
PC or changing control-flow resolution.

and load exclusion. CoreMark's dependency-heavy integer code exercises these
paths constantly, and the previous one-hot forwarding attempt showed that a
broader interface rewrite can regress, so the safer angle is a local factoring

title: Split RVFI shadow pipeline
motivation:
The strongest structural wins for this core have come from removing existing
fanout and packed pipeline routing without adding prediction, buffering, or
new stall policy.  Stage-local control bundles were a large improvement, but
the execution stage payloads still carry many RVFI-only fields through the
same packed structs and hold/reset paths used by the hot ALU, forwarding,
memory, and writeback logic.  Prior single-field cleanup was too QoR-sensitive,
so this should be treated as a deliberate structural split rather than another
isolated payload deletion.

title: Split RVFI order counter carry
motivation:
The single-issue FPGA wrapper deliberately XOR-reduces every RVFI output into
the LED, so retirement bookkeeping is kept in the synthesized timing model
even though it does not affect CoreMark execution. The current top level
increments `rvfi_order_q` with a full 64-bit `+ 1` every retiring cycle. That
carry chain is a plausible Fmax limiter after the larger ALU, control-bundle,
and regfile-reset optimizations have already landed.

title: Multicycle high-half multiply side unit
motivation:
The current best core still keeps one sign-selectable 33x33 multiplier in the
common combinational ALU so MUL, MULH, MULHU, and MULHSU can all complete in a
single EX cycle. Prior history showed that sharing the multiplier was a small
win, while moving rare DIV/REM work into a side unit was a large structural
win once it reused a disciplined EX-busy stall path. CoreMark is much more
likely to need low-half MUL than high-half multiply variants, so the high-half
cases can afford extra latency if the common EX arithmetic cone becomes
smaller and easier to route.

title: Forward load data to stores
motivation:
The current hazard unit still treats a store's rs2 data operand as an EX-stage
consumer for load-use interlocking. That is conservative for correctness, but
a store does not actually need its write data until MEM, one cycle later, when
the preceding load has already reached MEM/WB. CoreMark has frequent memory
traffic, so removing this specific load-to-store data bubble can improve CPI
without touching the fetch predictor, branch redirect, ALU multiplier, or the
failed same-cycle dmem-to-EX load bypass path.

motivation:
